coredata.dc:title: Thermally activated delayed fluorescence fluorescent probe based on triazine as emission core for metal ions detection
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Suyu
Yu, Jianmin
Zhou, Tao
Zhang, Kaizhi
Duan, Yu
Ban, Xinxin
Zhu, Qingzheng
Shi, Linxing
Zhang, Dongen
coredata.dc:description: A novel thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) sensor PhTRZ-CzOCHO was designed and synthesized with triazine ring as the receptor and carbazole as the donor group. Low temperature dependent photoluminescence spectra shows that PhTRZ-CzOCHO has a small singlet and triplet energy splitting (ΔEST = 0.25 eV), which ensures the efficient reverse intersystem crossing. The intensity of the fluorescence was three times higher after removing oxygen, which also proves the triplet involved TADF process. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of PhTRZ-CzOCHO was calculated to be 58%. The delayed fluorescence lifetime of the probe was 0.32 μs. Moreover, the fluorescence titration measurement demonstrates that this TADF probe can recognize most of metal ions due to its strong complexing ability of aldehyde group. The detection limitation of Na+, Mg2+ and Fe3+were calculated to be 7.03 × 10−7 mol/L, 6.7 × 10−7 mol/L and 5.9 × 10−8 mol/L, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of TADF probe to test most of metal ions, which may have potential implications for environmental and biological monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TADF
Fluorescence probe
Metal ions
Energy splitting

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for the Grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21805106), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20181073), the Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province (17KJB150007), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (1107040175), and the Science Foundation of Huaihai Institute of Technology (KQ16025, Z2016010). The First-class Undergraduate Majors Construction Program of Jiangsu Province , the Key Discipline Construction Program of Jiangsu Province , a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions ( PAPD ). The Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX20_2920). We also thank for a project funded by the priority academic program development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions ( PAPD ) and the project supported by Scientific and Technological Program of Lianyungang (JC1603, CG1602). Lianyungang Haiyan Plan (2018-QD-019). Six talent peaks project in Jiangsu Province(JNHB 114).

We are grateful for the Grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21805106), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20181073), the Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province (17KJB150007), the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (1107040175), and the Science Foundation of Huaihai Institute of Technology (KQ16025, Z2016010). The First-class Undergraduate Majors Construction Program of Jiangsu Province, the Key Discipline Construction Program of Jiangsu Province, a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD). The Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX20_2920). We also thank for a project funded by the priority academic program development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) and the project supported by Scientific and Technological Program of Lianyungang (JC1603, CG1602). Lianyungang Haiyan Plan (2018-QD-019). Six talent peaks project in Jiangsu Province(JNHB 114).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) DSC curve of probe recorded at heating rate of 10 °C min−1. (b) TGA analysis of PhTRZ-CzOCHO recorded at heating rate of 10 °C min−1.
Cyclic voltammogram of probe dissolved in DCM.
(a)The fluorescence spectra of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 and PhTRZ-CzOCHO. (b) The phosphorescence (77 K) spectra of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 and PhTRZ-CzOCHO.

(a) The room temperature normalized UV–vis absorption spectra in different solutions. (b) PL spectra of PhTRZ-CzOCHO in different solvents. (c) Lipperte −Mataga plot of PHTRZ-CzOCHO in various solutions. (d) Fluorescence spectra of PhTRZ-CzOCHO in oxygen and nitrogen. (e) Transient photoluminescence decay curve.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of PHTRZ-CzOCHO in solvent with different water content. (b) Fluorescence spectra of PHTRZ-CzOCHO in different PH.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of probe with different metal ions. (b) Fluorescence spectra of ion quenching histogram. (c) Ions quenching real image。 (d) Ions response to PhTRZ-CzOCH3。.

(a) Fluorescence response of PhTRZ-CzOCHO with titration of Na+ concentration.(b) Linear relationship between fluorescence response at 470 nm of PhTRZ-CzOCHO and Na+. (c) Job's plot curve of PhTRZ-CzOCHO and Na+. (d) Fluorescence response of PhTRZ-CzOCHO with titration of Mg2+ concentration. (e) Linear relationship between fluorescence response at 470 nm of PhTRZ-CzOCHO and Mg2+. (f) Job's plot curve of PhTRZ-CzOCHO and Mg2+. (g) Fluorescence response of PhTRZ-CzOCHO with titration of Fe3+ concentration.(h) Linear relationship between fluorescence response at 470 nm of PhTRZ-CzOCHO and Fe3+. (i) Job's plot curve of PhTRZ-CzOCHO and Fe3+.
Infrared spectra of PhTRZ-CzOCH3, PhTRZ-CzOH and PhTRZ-CzOCHO.
Schematic diagram of probe response to metal ions.
Probe test paper (a) Original test paper (b) quenched by adding Fe3+.
Spectral properties of probe in different solvents.

head.ce:title: Thermally activated delayed fluorescence fluorescent probe based on triazine as emission core for metal ions detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiansu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment
School of Marine Life and Fisheries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiansu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment, School of Marine Life and Fisheries, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang, Jiangsu, 222005, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) sensor PhTRZ-CzOCHO was designed and synthesized with triazine ring as the receptor and carbazole as the donor group. Low temperature dependent photoluminescence spectra shows that PhTRZ-CzOCHO has a small singlet and triplet energy splitting (ΔEST = 0.25 eV), which ensures the efficient reverse intersystem crossing. The intensity of the fluorescence was three times higher after removing oxygen, which also proves the triplet involved TADF process. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of PhTRZ-CzOCHO was calculated to be 58%. The delayed fluorescence lifetime of the probe was 0.32 μs. Moreover, the fluorescence titration measurement demonstrates that this TADF probe can recognize most of metal ions due to its strong complexing ability of aldehyde group. The detection limitation of Na+, Mg2+ and Fe3+were calculated to be 7.03 × 10−7 mol/L, 6.7 × 10−7 mol/L and 5.9 × 10−8 mol/L, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of TADF probe to test most of metal ions, which may have potential implications for environmental and biological monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared with traditional detection methods, fluorescent probes has been widely used in fluorescence sensors, biological probes, biological imaging and other fields rely on the characteristics of simple synthesis method, fast detection speed, high sensitivity and low dependence [1–5]. Fluorescence detection technology has become a powerful tool for detecting neutral and ionic substances with its excellent photophysical and photochemical properties by detecting the structural changes in the microscopic field of the probe molecule and the tested sample [6–8]. In recent years, the synthesis of fluorescent chemical sensor has received wide attention from scientific researchers for its influence on the environment and health [9,10]. Alkali metal ions were involved in the regulation of various physiological functions in the organism. What's more, the indicators of the content of various metal ions in the human body have become a important criteria for judging human health [11,12]. Currently, many metal ion detection fluorescent probes have been reported, Lu Li et al. have established a reaction-based turn-off fluorescence chemosensor which was used to detect Na+ and K+ based on the heterocyclic structure of BODIPY can coordinate with metal ions. The probe can coordinate 1:1 with Na+ or K+ relied on its superb binding ability, together with the detection limits were 2.5 μM−1 and 2.7 μM−1, respectively [13]. As described in the literature [14], the conventional fluorescence sensor can achieve a certain qualitative and quantitative detection of some metal ions which were tested. Conventional fluorescence sensor was used to detect some metal ions based on the change of fluorescence. However, when the traditional fluorescent probe was used for biological imaging, it would be seriously interfered by the body's own luminescence [15].

Compared with the shorter fluorescence lifetime of traditional fluorescence, the longer fluorescence lifetime of thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) material has attracted the interest of scientific researchers [16,17]. Huiyi Yin et al. synthesized the first TADF sensor which can be used for sulfite photoluminescence imaging in living cells based on mechanism of acetylated luciferin on sulfite removal. In order to overcome the background fluorescence of the lifetime fluorescent protein and the interference of scattered light, the luminescence lifetime distribution of this chemical sensor was capable of achieving 14 μs depended on the long luminescence lifetime of TADF compounds to realize the fluorescence detection of photoluminescence lifetime imaging in living cells [18]. Since TADF materials have small energy splitting (ΔEST), excitons at the triplet energy level (T1) can be converted into singlet energy level (S1) through reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) and then emit photons by delayed fluorescence [19–22]. The delayed fluorescence of TADF materials were able to make up for the shortcomings of traditional fluorescence sensors in that the fluorescence lifetime of these materials could only reach the level of nanosecond [23–25]. Delayed fluorescence can eliminate the interference of background fluorescence and scattered light of short-lived fluorescent proteins, thereby getting more clear molecular imaging [26–28].

For solving the issue in short fluorescence lifetime of traditional fluorescence sensors, in this work, we introduced TADF structure to optimize the fluorescence sensor relying on its delayed fluorescence. The TADF fluorescent sensor PhTRZ-CzOCHO were designed and synthesized by using triazine and carbazole as charge transfer emissive core, and aldehyde group as the recognition group of the probe to identify and detect ions. Photophysical measurements prove that the probe has obvious recognition effect on different valence metal ions even the monovalent alkali metals, which can only be tested by crown ether compounds in previous reports. Transient PL test indicates the probe has a fluorescence lifetime of 0.32 μs. Through electrochemical calculations, the separation of HOMO and LUMO orbitals makes the strong charge transfer characteristics and a small ΔEST. Thermodynamic test shows that the probe has good stability, the Tg and Td of the probes were 154 °C and 450 °C, respectively. As far as we know, this is the first report on TADF fluorescent probes for metal ions, which is expected to be used as biological fluorescent imaging probe relies on its long fluorescence lifetime.

All reactants and solvents, unless otherwise stated, were purchased from commercial sources and used as received. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were measured on a BRUKER AMX 300- or 500- MHz instrument with tetramethylsilane as the internal standard. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curves were scanned on STA 409 PC and DSC 2910 modulated calorimeter. The film surface morphology was measured with AFM (Seiko Instruments, SPA-400). Uv–vis absorption spectra were obtained by SHIMADZU Uv-2450, PL spectrum were received by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jobin Yvon, FluoroMax-3). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed on a CHI750C voltammetric analyzer. Infrared spectrum was derived from TENSOR 27.
Scheme 1 showed the synthetic routes and structure of the newly synthesized PhTRZ-CzOCHO. Firstly, 2,4,6-tris(3-bromophenyl)-triazine (TRZ) was synthesized according to the literature procedure [29].

A round-bottom flask was charged with TRZ (2 g, 3.66 mmol), phenylboronic acid (0.9 g, 7.4 mmol), tetrakis (triphenylphosphine) palladium (0.04 g, 0.03 mmol) were added to a round bottom flask with THF (50 ml). Na2CO3 was dissolved in deionized water (5 mL) to prepare 6 M sodium carbonate solution. The reaction was stirred at 75 °C for 12 h under the protection of N2 and observed by thin-layer chromatography (TLC). THF was dislodged by rotary evaporator, and then the organic phase was extracted with dichloromethane. The disubstituted products separated by column chromatography (petroleum ether: dichloromethane = 5:1). Finally, the products were dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 5 h to obtain the pure white product PhTRZ (0.7 g, 35%). 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3) δ 8.97 (t, J = 1.6 Hz, 2H), 8.89 (t, J = 1.7 Hz, 1H), 8.77–8.70 (m, 3H), 7.87–7.82 (m, 2H), 7.78–7.72 (m, 5H), 7.66 (t, J = 7.7 Hz, 2H), 7.53 (dd, J = 10.5, 4.8 Hz, 4H), 7.48–7.40 (m, 3H).

To solution of PhTRZ (0.6 g, 1.1 mmol) and 4-methoxycarbazole (0.22 g, 1.1 mmol) were dissolved in a round bottom flask of dehydrated DMF. K2CO3 (0.48 g, 3.45 mmol), CuI (0.42 g, 2.21 mmol) and 1,10-Phenanthroline monohydrate (0.04 g, 0.2 mmol) were added to the mixture and stirred at 160 °C for 24 h in the N2 environment. TLC was performed to observe the reaction until completed, then the reaction was stopped and cooled to room temperature. Deionized water and dichloromethane were used to extract the organic phase, and the products were purified by column chromatography (petroleum ether: dichloromethane = 4:1). Finally, the products were dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 5 h to obtain the pale yellow product PhTRZ-CzOCH3 (0.63 g, 87%). 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3) δ 9.00 (dd, J = 7.2, 5.6 Hz, 3H), 8.88–8.84 (m, 1H), 8.76–8.71 (m, 2H), 8.45 (d, J = 7.7 Hz, 1H), 7.83 (dd, J = 6.6, 4.3, 1.9 Hz, 4H), 7.74–7.69 (m, 4H), 7.63 (t, J = 7.7 Hz, 2H), 7.50 (dd, J = 14.6, 7.4 Hz, 5H), 7.41 (tdd, J = 8.5, 6.1, 2.3 Hz, 3H), 7.37–7.31 (m, 2H), 7.14 (d, J = 8.2 Hz, 1H), 6.78 (d, J = 7.9 Hz, 1H), 4.14 (s, 3H).

A round bottemed flask was charged with PhTRZ-CzOCH3 (0.5 g, 0.76 mmol) and CH2Cl2 (10 mL). The reaction system was kept under ice bath conditions, together with boron tribromide (0.4 mL) and dichloromethane (10 mL) were placed in a dry constant pressure dropping funnel and added dropwise. Then stirred at room temperature for 6 h after the system stabilized. The reaction system was neutralized with sat. NaHCO3 aq., and then the organic layer was extracted by using DCM. The product was washed by deionized water and recrystallized with petroleum ether and dichloromethane. Finally, the products were dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 5 h to obtain the pale orange product PhTRZ-CzOH (0.46 g, 92%). 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3) δ 9.00 (d, J = 9.9 Hz, 3H), 8.89–8.83 (m, 1H), 8.73 (d, J = 7.8 Hz, 2H), 8.41 (d, J = 7.7 Hz, 1H), 7.81 (q, J = 8.2 Hz, 4H), 7.71 (d, J = 7.4 Hz, 4H), 7.62 (t, J = 7.7 Hz, 2H), 7.54–7.46 (m, 5H), 7.45–7.37 (m, 3H), 7.35 (t, J = 7.4 Hz, 1H), 7.29–7.25 (m, 1H), 7.11 (d, J = 8.2 Hz, 1H), 6.67 (d, J = 7.7 Hz, 1H).

PhTRZ-CzOH (0.4 g, 0.62 mmol) and p-fluorobenzaldehyde (0.008 g, 0.064 mmol) dissolved in DMSO (10 ml) were taken into a round-bottom flask. K2CO3 (0.024 g, 0.17 mmol) was added into the flask after solid stirred and dissolved. The resultant mixture was stirred at 160 °C for 12 h in the N2 environment. TLC was used to observe the reaction until complete, and the reaction was stopped and cooled down to room temperature. After that, The reaction system was poured into a large amount of deionized water, the filtered solid was dissolved with dichloromethane, the product was purified by column chromatography (petroleum ether: dichloromethane = 3:1), and finally dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 5 h to obtain light yellow product PhTRZ-CzOCHO (0.4 g, 87%). 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3) δ 9.94 (s, 1H), 9.04 (s, 1H), 9.00 (t, J = 1.6 Hz, 2H), 8.90 (dd, J = 7.8, 4.9, 1.9 Hz, 1H), 8.74 (d, J = 7.8 Hz, 2H), 8.04 (d, J = 7.9 Hz, 1H), 7.92–7.88 (m, 2H), 7.84 (dd, J = 9.7, 3.2 Hz, 4H), 7.73–7.69 (m, 4H), 7.64 (t, J = 7.7 Hz, 2H), 7.55–7.37 (m, 11H), 7.24–7.20 (m, 2H), 7.00–6.96 (m, 1H). 13C NMR (126 MHz, CDCl3) δ 190.83, 171.93, 170.98, 163.14, 150.03, 142.92, 141.76, 140.71, 140.66, 138.51, 137.99, 136.45, 132.21, 131.50, 131.37, 130.93, 130.40, 129.22, 128.94, 128.27, 127.97, 127.70, 127.68, 127.56, 127.29, 126.90, 126.38, 123.07, 121.31, 120.75, 117.24, 116.10, 111.67, 109.68, 106.85.

Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and thermogravimetry (TGA) were used to study the thermal properties of the PhTRZ-CzOCHO powder samples. Certain amount of PhTRZ-CzOCHO powder sample was heated to 800 °C in a nitrogen atmosphere from room temperature (26 °C) at a rate of 10 °C min−1. The TGA and DSC data records were shown in Fig. 1 . It can be seen from Fig. 1 that the Tg and Td of the probe molecule were 154 °C and 450 °C respectively, which indicated that the PhTRZ-CzOCHO molecule had a good thermal stability. This was related to the non-planar structure and higher molecular weight of PhTRZ-CzOCHO. Excellent thermal stability may benefit to improve the morphological stability and long-term reliability of the fluorescence sensor.

In order to further understand the film-forming properties of PhTRZ-CzOCHO, atomic force microscope (AFM) were utilized to test and analyze the probe spin coating film. As shown in Fig. 2 , the AFM topography image PhTRZ-CzOCHO was quite smooth and uniform for the smaller value of root mean square roughness, which roughness was 0.631 nm, without crack and aggregation. Relatively surface can be coated with a uniform test film, which is helpful for optimizing the sensitivity of the fluorescent probe.

The electrochemical properties of PhTRZ-CzOCHO in dichloromethane were studied by cyclic voltammetry using ferrocene as reference material. Test curve was demonstrated in Fig. 3 , the initial oxidation potential peak of the probe was 0.91 V. According to the following formula: HOMO = −(EOX - 0.4 + Efe) eV, the HOMO energy level of probe is calculated to be −5.38 eV. Among them, EOX and Efe were the oxidation potential of the probe material itself and the oxidation-reduction potential of ferrocene, respectively.

In order to further understand the photophysical properties of fluorescent probes, density functional theory (DFT) was used to study and compare the electrochemical and geometric properties of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 and PhTRZ-CzOCHO. As can be seen from Fig. 4 , the carbazoles increased the degree of spatial distortion of the triazine ring, effectively reducing the intermolecular π-π accumulation. The HOMO and LUMO level of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 are located on the triazine ring and carbazole unit, while PhTRZ-CzOCHO have a similar energy level distribution to PhTRZ-CzOCH3. On the basis of theoretical calculation, the HOMO/LUMO value of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 was −5.18 eV/-1.95 eV, and the HOMU/LUMO value of PhTRZ-CzOCHO was −5.55 eV/-2.07 eV. Since HOMO and LUMO orbitals overlapped slightly on the connected benzene ring so that the separation of HOMO and LUMO orbitals makes smaller ΔEST. To further simulate the emission characteristics, TD-DTF was used to calculate the exciton energy of the probe. The energy of the PhTRZ-CzOCH3 singlet and triplet states were 2.76 eV and 2.65 eV, respectively as well as the PhTRZ-CzOCHO were 3.01 eV and 2.83 eV, respectively. Therefore, the ΔEST of PHTRZ-CzOCH3 and PHTRZ-CzOCHO were 0.11 eV and 0.18 eV, respectively. As it can be seen that the introduction of the recognition group aldehyde group had no significant effect on ΔEST. Both of two probes have smaller ΔEST, which were conducive to the reverse intersystem crossing from T1 level to S1 level and emitted delayed fluorescence to achieve a better detection effect.

To compared the fluorescence peaks of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 and PhTRZ-CzOCHO. Fig. 5 a showed that the fluorescence emission peak of PhTRZ-CzOCHO is 470 nm, which belongs to blue luminescent material. The fluorescence emission peak of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 is 490 nm, which belongs to green light category. Thus, the smaller excitation wavelength was, the more energy which can provide to promote electron transition and thus enhance the emitted delayed fluorescence. Meanwhile, the corresponding energy levels were calculated by the formula as follow:
Eg =
hv λ
=
1240 λ

According to the tangent line of fluorescence emission siderband, the single energies of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 and PhTRZ-CzOCHO can be calculated as 2.92 eV and 3.02 eV, respectively. Fig. 5b exhibited the phosphorescence spectra of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 and PhTRZ-CzOCHO at 77 K in THF. Based on the first peak of phosphorescence emission, the triplet energies of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 and PhTRZ-CzOCHO were 2.79 eV and 2.81 eV, respectively. The ΔEST values of PhTRZ-CzOCH3 and PhTRZ-CzOCHO were 0.17 eV and 0.25 eV, respectively, which were consistent with the theoretical calculation.

To explore the physical properties of probe in different solvents, the probe PhTRZ-CzOCHO was dissolved in four solvents respectively to compare differences with ultraviolet and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the probe has a strong absorption peak attributed to the n-π at 290 nm, together with 325 nm and 340 nm belong to π-π* absorption peak and CT absorption peak of the probe, respectively. In addition, the energy gap value of PHTRZ-CzOCHO can be obtained from the initial absorption band as 3.42 eV. Table 1 and Fig. 6b showed that the probe has a slight red shift in methylbenzene, ethyl acetate and tetrahydrofuran, which belong to the blue light range, while the probe has a red shift to the green light range in DMF. TADF material tend to be highly polar, and an electric field similar to a solvent usually stabilizes its excited state. As the polarity of the solvent increases, the spontaneous state of CT would decrease and this stabilizing effect get much stronger. Thanks to the transfer of intramolecular charge from carbazole donor to triazine receptor, there is a large dipole moment in the excited state. Therefore, the energy of the polar solvent interacting with the compound decreases in the more stable excited state, leading to the emission red-shift [30]. Fig. 6c demonstrated that the study for relationship between the changes of molecular dipole moment and directional polarization in excited states, expressed stokes shift by Lipper-Mataga equation as follows:
(1)
h c
(
ν a
−
ν f
)
= h c
(
ν a 0
−
ν f 0
)
−
2
(
μ e
−
μ g
)
2
a 3
f
(
ε , n
)
(2)
f
(
ε , n
)
=
ε − 1
2 ε + 1
−
n 2
− 1
2
n 2
+ 1

According to the above formula, the slope value of the fitting line is 9523.15 cm−1, and the dipole moment is 10.73 D. A small dipole moment is conducive to the ICT effect, which makes the probe less susceptible to the influence of a small amount of solvent in other environments when the detection range is expanded and the application environment is applied. It can be seen in Fig. 6d, the fluorescence intensity test of probe PhTRZ-CzOCHO in air and nitrogen. The probe solution was sealed in a colorimeter and pumped with nitrogen for 5 min, and then the fluorescence intensity was tested. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the probe in nitrogen environment was higher than in oxygen which was owing to the oxygen in the air can quench the triplet excitons to a large extent, so the integral fluorescence was enhanced 3.2 times in the inert gas environment which demonstrate the probe had the characteristics of TADF. To prove that PhTRZ-CzOCHO has the characteristics of TADF, the fluorescence lifetime of the probe is measured by transient PL. Fig. 6e was the transient PL decay curve, and the transient delayed fluorescence lifetime can be fitted by the formula as following:
I
( t )
=
A 1
exp
(
− t
/
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
(
− t
/
τ 2
)

According to the transient PL diagram and the fitting formula, the transient fluorescence lifetime and the delayed fluorescence lifetime of the probe were 24 ns and 0.32 μs, respectively. Delayed fluorescence can be attributed to the transition of excitons from triplet state to singlet state through RISC, which indicates the TADF property.

Fig. 7 a exhibited that the fluorescence spectra of probe PhTRZ-CzOCHO in different proportions of THF mixed with water. Gradually increased the proportion of water in the solvent, and when the water content reaches 50%, the fluorescence intensity reaches the minimum. The reason was the change in the polarity of the solution resulting in reducing of fluorescence intensity. However, after 50%, the fluorescence intensity gradually enhanced with the increase of water content. When the water content reached 85%, it returned into the initial fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity achieved its maximum when the water content reaches 100% (Fig. S1). Thanks to the probe PhTRZ-CzOCHO molecules in a high water content stated to form a molecular aggregate, along with inhibition of the free rotation of molecules resulted in enhanced fluorescence. Owing to the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect of TADF probes in aqueous solution, such probes can have a wider detection range.

In order to test the stability of probe PhTRZ-CzOCHO in different PH environments. Probe solution was added to a series of different PH (3–13) buffers. After 5 min, the fluorescence intensity of the probe at 470 nm was measured. As is shown in Fig. 7b that the fluorescence intensity had a slight downward trend with the increase of PH with no great fluctuation (Fig. S2). Finally, PH = 7 was chosen as the next test condition.

As shown in Fig. 8 a, the probe had a strong fluorescence at 470 nm, but when metal ions were added, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply and even was quenched. The fluorescence peak was accompanied by a slight red shift to 479 nm, as the addition of water changed the polarity of the solvent. As it can be seen in Fig. 8b and c that +1, +2 and + 3 valence metal ions have strong fluorescence weakening effect on the probe, while Pb2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ quenched the probe almost completely. Na+, Mg2+ and Fe3+ were selected from +1, +2 and + 3 valence metal ions for fluorescence titration test to further response relationship of probe to them. As can be seen from Fig. 8d, there was almost no PL response when Na+, Mg2+ and Fe3+ ions were added to PhTRZ-CzOCH3, which indicates the methoxy group is not the identifying group. This further proves that the aldehyde group is an effective group for recognizing metal ions after the introduction of aldehyde group.

Fluorescence titration was used to determine the fluorescence intensity of the probe and sodium ions. Fig. 9 a demonstrated that Na+ had obvious fluorescence reduction effect on probe. As it can be seen from Fig. 9b, the linear equation of Na+ identification by probe was obtained. The detection limit is calculated by formula LOD = 3δ/K, and the detection line was 7.03 × 10−7 mol/L. Fig. 9c exhibited that the binding ratio of probe to Na+ was calculated as 1:4 by the jobs work curve. When the ratio of probe to Na+ was 1:4, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum. Similarly, the complexation ratio of Mg2+ and Fe3+ to the probe were calculated to be 4:1. The detection limits of Mg2+ and Fe3+ were 6.7 × 10−7 mol/L and 5.9 × 10−8 mol/L, respectively.

As we can see from Fig. 10 , the infrared radiation (IR) demonstrated that the C–O stretching vibration peak at 1090 cm−1 disappeared and the –OH stretching vibration peak at 3460 cm−1 increased from PhTRZ-CzOCH3 to PhTRZ-CzOH which demonstrated a successful transition from methoxy to hydroxyl. The new characteristic peak of PhTRZ-CzOCHO at 1690 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of CO which indicated the successful introduction of the aldehyde group.

Fig. 11 Showed that the addition of metal ions and the aldehyde group form complexes, which prevent intramolecular charge transfer and lead to fluorescence quenching. As demonstrated in Fig. 12 , two groups of probe test paper were prepared which has strong luminescence under ultraviolet lamp. After Fe3+ solution (10−6 M) was dripped on one group which can be seen quenched obviously. The experiments showed that the probe test paper was successfully prepared.

In this paper, we have successfully synthesized a novel type of TADF fluorescent probe for detecting metal ions. The integral fluorescence intensity was enhanced by three times higher in the absence of oxygen. The sensitivity to oxygen demonstrates the TADF property. The transient fluorescence lifetime and the delayed fluorescence lifetime were 24 ns and 0.32 μs, respectively, which also proves the probe PhTRZ-CzOCHO has the characteristic of TADF. Thanks to its bulky molecular structure, the probe possessed a high thermal and film forming ability. The AFM testing showed that the roughness of the membrane was only 0.631 nm. The optical properties of PhTRZ-CzOCHO were studied, and the results showed that PhTRZ-CzOCHO had strong complexing ability to recognize almost all metal ions. Among them, the detection limits of Na+, Mg2+ and Fe3+ metal ions were 7.03 × 10−7 mol/L, 6.7 × 10−7 mol/L and 5.9 × 10−8 mol/L, respectively. Job's work curve demonstrated that the complexation ratios of probe to Na+, Mg2+ and Fe3+ were 4:1, 1:4 and 1:4, respectively. In addition, the fluorescence spectrum of the probe gradually red-shifted with the increase of solvent polarity which performed a strong intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect, and the dipole moment was 10.73 D. The probe was expected to be a fluorescence imaging sensor for living organisms in that it can provide a high imaging resolution and accurate data analysis.

Suyu Qiu: Scheme Design, Probe Synthesis, Writing – original draft. Jianmin Yu: Theoretical calculation. Tao Zhou: Formal analysis. Kaizhi Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Yu Duan: Writing – review & editing. Xinxin Ban: Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Qingzheng Zhu: Writing – review & editing. Linxing Shi: Writing – review & editing. Dongen Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The First-class Undergraduate Majors Construction Program of Jiangsu Province, the Key Discipline Construction Program of Jiangsu Province, a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD). The Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX20_2920). We also thank for a project funded by the priority academic program development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) and the project supported by Scientific and Technological Program of Lianyungang (JC1603, CG1602). Lianyungang Haiyan Plan (2018-QD-019). Six talent peaks project in Jiangsu Province(JNHB 114).
